1: The LORD reigns! — the proclamation YHWH malak (or YHWH malakh) is the enthronement formula that identifies Psalms 93, 96, 97, 98, and 99 as the Enthronement Psalms. The formula has been understood either as a present tense declaration ("YHWH is king") or as a performative enthronement cry ("YHWH has become king"). Book IV of the Psalter (Ps 90-106) gathers these enthronement psalms as the theological response to the Davidic monarchy's failure: even when the human king is gone, YHWH remains enthroned. The three-fold royal investiture formula — "clothed with majesty... clothed with strength... has girded himself" — is the formal declaration of divine kingship taking up its royal regalia.
2: Your throne has been established from ancient times; you yourself are from everlasting — the antiquity of YHWH's enthronement is not recent but pre-cosmic. "From ancient times" (me'az) and "from everlasting" (me'olam) locate the divine throne in the pre-creation order. Unlike human thrones that are established through conquest and can be overthrown, YHWH's throne was established before the world existed — it is the precondition of the world, not the world's product. This has a direct bearing on the exilic situation: the Babylonian king's apparent victory over Jerusalem and its institutions does not displace the eternal divine throne.
3: The great waters have lifted up their voice, LORD; the rushing floods have lifted up their voice and their crashing waves — the personified chaos waters raising their voices against YHWH. In ancient Near Eastern cosmology (particularly the Ugaritic Baal cycle), the primordial waters (Yam, the sea) were the great enemy that the storm god had to defeat before he could be enthroned as king. The Psalter consistently identifies YHWH (not Baal) as the true conqueror of the chaos waters (Ps 29:10; 74:12-14; 89:9-10). The threefold repetition of "have lifted up" (nasa'u) — voice, voice, crashing — imitates the relentless, wave-like nature of the challenge.
4: Mightier than the roar of many waters, mightier than the waves of the sea — the LORD on high is mighty — the answer to v3's challenge: YHWH is addiri (mighty, excellent, majestic) beyond the sum of all the waters' power. The repetition of "mightier than" frames the psalm's central claim: every power that could challenge divine order is weaker than YHWH. This has eschatological resonance: Revelation uses the voice of many waters as an image of both threatening power (Rev 17:15 — the waters where the Harlot sits are peoples and nations) and divine victory (Rev 14:2; 19:6 — the voice of many waters as the sound of YHWH's triumph).
5: Your decrees are completely trustworthy; holiness adorns your house, LORD, forevermore — the transition from cosmic to covenantal. The same YHWH who overcame chaos-waters has also given trustworthy edot (decrees, testimonies — the covenant stipulations). The reliability of divine decrees in history is the moral equivalent of his victory over chaos in creation: the same sovereign power that established the stable world also established a morally ordered covenant. "Holiness adorns your house" — the temple as the meeting point of YHWH's cosmic kingship and his covenantal presence. The "forevermore" (le'orekh yamim) echoes the eternal enthronement of v2: both throne and house are permanent.
